title: Visual Disorders, Glasses, Contact Lenses and Eye Surgery, Part B
subtitle: Correction technologies, matching them to patients, and the traps
subject: Biology
syllabusDotPoints: explain a range of causes of disorders by investigating the structures and functions of the relevant organs, for example the eye
investigate technologies that are used to assist with the effects of a disorder, including spectacle lenses, contact lenses and corrective surgery (ACSBL100)
inquiryQuestion: How can technologies be used to assist people who experience disorders?
lessonIntent: Students can compare glasses, contact lenses and LASIK, match a correction to a patient, and explain why LASIK does not prevent presbyopia.
examSkill: Choose and justify glasses, contacts or LASIK for a given patient, and avoid the common refractive-correction traps.
productionNotes: Part B of a 2-part split of Lesson 19. Opens with a brief recap of Part A, then covers the correction technologies, a worked example, the traps, a quick-check and the summary. Assumes the student has watched Part A.

introVoiceover.text: Welcome to HSC Science. This is HSC Biology. Today, visual disorders, continued, the correction technologies and how to apply them. Let's go.

scenes.caption: Visual Disorders, Glasses, Contact Lenses and Eye Surgery, Part B (Year 12 Biology, Module 8, Lesson 19)

scenes.heading: Where we left off
scenes.body: Quick recap from Part A: the cornea does most of the focusing, the lens accommodates, and each refractive error has a matching lens.

bullets.text: Cornea provides ~70% of focusing power; the lens fine-tunes by changing shape.
Myopia focuses in front, hyperopia behind, astigmatism at multiple points.
Myopia concave, hyperopia convex, astigmatism cylindrical.

scenes.callout: Now let's correct it with technology.
scenes.caption: Recap of Part A: the optics, the three errors, and which lens fixes which.

voiceover.text: Welcome back. Let's quickly recap Part A before we go on. The cornea provides about seventy percent of the eye's focusing power with a fixed curvature, while the flexible lens fine-tunes focus by changing shape, which is accommodation. The three refractive errors are myopia, where light focuses in front of the retina, hyperopia, where it focuses behind, and astigmatism, where an uneven cornea gives multiple focal points. And the matching lenses are concave for myopia, convex for hyperopia, and cylindrical for astigmatism. With that locked in, let's now correct these errors with real technology.

scenes.heading: Glasses, contacts and LASIK
scenes.body: All three corrections move the focal point onto the retina, but they differ in where they act and whether they can be undone.

bullets.text: Glasses sit in front of the eye, are fully reversible, and suit all ages.
Contact lenses sit on the cornea, giving better peripheral vision but an infection risk.
LASIK reshapes the cornea with a laser, permanent and effective but irreversible.

scenes.secondary: LASIK uses a laser to remove precise corneal tissue, flattening the centre for myopia or steepening it for hyperopia. About 95% of patients reach 6/6 vision, but the corneal tissue is gone for good, and it still does not stop presbyopia later.
scenes.callout: Glasses reversible, contacts closer, LASIK permanent.
scenes.caption: Glasses are external and reversible; contacts sit on the cornea; LASIK permanently reshapes the cornea.

voiceover.text: Now the three technologies, and they all do the same optical job, moving the focal point onto the retina, but they differ in where they act, their risk, and whether they can be undone. First, glasses, which place a corrective lens in front of the eye. They're fully reversible, you just take them off, they carry essentially no ocular risk, and they suit all ages, which makes them first-line for children, and they're the best option for presbyopia using bifocals or progressives. Second, contact lenses, which sit directly on the tear film over the cornea. Because they move with the eye, they give better peripheral vision than glasses, but sitting on the cornea brings a real risk, corneal infection if they're worn too long or cleaned poorly, plus the cornea can be starved of oxygen. Third, LASIK, which permanently reshapes the cornea using an excimer laser. A thin flap is lifted, then the laser vaporises precise amounts of corneal tissue, flattening the centre to correct myopia, or steepening it to correct hyperopia, and the flap is laid back without stitches. About ninety five percent of patients reach six-six vision, but here's the catch, the corneal tissue is removed permanently, so it's irreversible, and crucially it still doesn't prevent presbyopia developing later.

scenes.heading: Matching correction to patient
scenes.question: Identify the disorder and best correction for each: (a) Emma, 17, sees her phone clearly but not the whiteboard, prescription -3.50; (b) Robert, 46, has perfect distance vision but now holds his phone further away to read; (c) Priya, 28, a competitive swimmer with a stable +2.00 prescription who finds glasses impractical.
scenes.coachNote: Name the disorder from the symptom pattern, then choose the correction that fits the cause and the person's life.
steps: (a) Emma's blur is distance-only with a minus prescription, so she has myopia; glasses or contacts with a concave lens fit, but not LASIK at 17 since her prescription may still change.
(b) Robert has perfect distance vision but failing near vision in his 40s, so this is presbyopia; convex reading glasses supply the lost near focus.
(c) Priya has a plus prescription, so hyperopia, corrected by a convex lens; as a swimmer, contacts or LASIK suit her better than glasses.
Each choice matches the disorder's cause and the patient's circumstances.
Answer: (a) myopia, concave glasses/contacts; (b) presbyopia, convex reading glasses; (c) hyperopia, contacts or LASIK ✓
scenes.caption: (a) myopia → concave; (b) presbyopia → convex reading glasses; (c) hyperopia → contacts or LASIK.

voiceover.text: Let's match correction to patient, because this is exactly how it's examined. Read the symptom pattern, name the disorder, then choose the fitting correction. Part a, Emma, seventeen, can read her phone clearly but can't see the whiteboard, with a prescription of minus three point five zero. Distance-only blur plus a minus, that's myopia, where the eye is too long. She needs a concave lens, in glasses or contacts, and notice, not LASIK, because at seventeen her prescription is likely still changing, and LASIK requires a stable prescription. Part b, Robert, forty six, with perfect distance vision but now holding his phone further away to read. Near vision failing in the mid-forties while distance stays perfect, that's the signature of presbyopia, the stiffening lens, not hyperopia. He needs convex reading glasses to supply the near focus his lens can no longer manage. Part c, Priya, twenty eight, a competitive swimmer with a stable plus two prescription who finds glasses impractical. A plus prescription means hyperopia, the eye too short, corrected by a convex lens. And given she's a swimmer, glasses are awkward, so contact lenses or, with a stable prescription at twenty eight, LASIK suit her far better. Match the cause and the lifestyle, every time.

scenes.heading: Three traps
scenes.body: Trap 1: thinking myopia means the lens is the wrong shape. In most cases the eyeball is simply too long, while the lens is normal.
scenes.secondary: Trap 2: believing LASIK fixes everything permanently, including reading. LASIK reshapes the cornea, but the lens still stiffens with age, so presbyopia still arrives. Trap 3: pairing the wrong lens with the disorder. A concave lens corrects myopia, not hyperopia, and a convex lens corrects hyperopia, never the reverse.
scenes.callout: Myopia is a long eyeball, LASIK won't stop presbyopia, concave for myopia.
scenes.caption: Myopia is usually axial length, not lens shape; LASIK doesn't prevent presbyopia; concave is for myopia.

voiceover.text: Three traps that quietly cost marks every year. Trap one, thinking myopia means the lens is the wrong shape. In most cases it isn't the lens at all, it's that the eyeball is too long axially, so the focal point falls short of the retina, while the lens itself is perfectly normal. Always cite axial length as the primary cause. Trap two, believing LASIK fixes everything permanently, including reading. LASIK reshapes the cornea, correcting your current myopia or hyperopia, but it has no effect at all on the crystalline lens. So the lens still stiffens with age, and presbyopia still develops, which is why LASIK patients in their forties still end up needing reading glasses. Trap three, the classic mix-up, pairing the wrong lens with the disorder. A concave, diverging lens corrects myopia, where the focus is too far forward, and a convex, converging lens corrects hyperopia, where the focus is too far back. Get those reversed and you lose the mark instantly, so remember, myopia concave, hyperopia convex. Nail those three and vision questions become easy marks.

scenes.heading: Quick check
scenes.question: A patient had LASIK for myopia at age 35 and gained perfect distance vision. Now at 55 he needs reading glasses and asks why, since he thought LASIK fixed his eyes permanently. Explain the biology, and state what LASIK did and did not correct.
answerSteps: LASIK permanently reshaped his cornea, flattening it to correct his myopia, and that distance correction still works.
LASIK acts only on the cornea; it has no effect on the crystalline lens.
By 55 his lens has hardened and lost elasticity, so it can no longer accommodate for near objects, which is presbyopia.
Presbyopia is a normal age-related change, independent of his earlier LASIK, so reading glasses supply the lost near focus.
Answer: LASIK corrected his corneal refractive error, not his lens; presbyopia is why he now needs reading glasses ✓
scenes.caption: LASIK fixed the cornea, not the lens; age-related presbyopia still requires reading glasses.

voiceover.text: Quick check, and this is a favourite exam scenario. A patient had LASIK for myopia at thirty five and gained perfect distance vision. Now at fifty five he needs reading glasses, and he's confused, because he thought LASIK fixed his eyes permanently. Explain the biology, and say clearly what LASIK did and did not correct. Pause and reason it through. Right, let's work it. First, LASIK did exactly what it was meant to, it permanently reshaped his cornea, flattening it to correct his myopia, and that distance correction is still working fine at fifty five. Second, and this is the key, LASIK acts only on the cornea. It has no effect whatsoever on the crystalline lens. Third, by fifty five his lens has naturally hardened and lost its elasticity, so it can no longer change shape to focus on near objects. That's presbyopia. Fourth, presbyopia is a normal, universal age-related change, completely independent of whether he ever had LASIK. So his reading difficulty isn't a LASIK failure, it's just lens aging, and convex reading glasses supply the near focus his stiff lens can no longer produce. LASIK corrected the cornea, not the lens.

scenes.heading: Lock it in
points: The cornea provides ~70% of focusing power; the lens accommodates by changing shape.
Myopia focuses in front of the retina, hyperopia behind, astigmatism at multiple points.
Myopia needs a concave lens, hyperopia a convex lens, astigmatism a cylindrical lens.
Glasses are reversible and suit all ages; contacts sit on the cornea; LASIK is permanent.
Presbyopia is a stiffening lens, not a length error, so LASIK does not prevent it.
scenes.finalPrompt: Given a vision problem? Find where the focus falls, name the cause, then pick the lens or surgery that moves it onto the retina.
scenes.caption: Five takeaways: the optics, the three errors, the lens types, the technologies, and presbyopia.

voiceover.text: Let's lock it in. Five things to take away. First, the cornea provides about seventy percent of the eye's focusing power with a fixed curvature, while the flexible lens fine-tunes focus by changing shape, which is accommodation. Second, the three refractive errors, myopia focuses light in front of the retina, hyperopia behind it, and astigmatism at multiple points because of an uneven cornea. Third, the matching lenses, myopia needs a concave lens, hyperopia needs a convex lens, and astigmatism needs a cylindrical lens. Fourth, the technologies, glasses are reversible and suit every age, contacts sit on the cornea for better peripheral vision but carry an infection risk, and LASIK permanently reshapes the cornea. And fifth, presbyopia is a stiffening lens with age, not a length error, which is why LASIK can't prevent it and reading glasses are still needed. So whenever you meet a vision problem, find where the focus falls, name the cause, then pick the lens or surgery that moves it back onto the retina. See you next lesson.
